error.signature: AI ignores Korean speech levels (존댓말/반말) when translating or drafting messages, collapsing everything to a single neutral register

verdict.summary: Korean speech levels are not stylistic preference — they encode social relationship. Using 반말 (banmal, plain form) with a superior, elder, or first-time contact is read as disrespect or aggression. Using overly stiff 하십시오체 with a close friend sounds cold. Email, customer service, and workplace messaging default to 해요체 or 합니다체; default 반말 output will be offensive.

dead_ends.action: Translate an English email to Korean as if the addressee were a peer, producing uniform 해 (해체/반말) endings
dead_ends.why_fails: The receiver parses 반말 from a stranger as either a machine-translation error or a pointed insult. Business communication requires at minimum 해요체 (-요 endings) and often the more formal 합니다체 (-ㅂ니다). Messages in 반말 to a boss, older colleague, or client can end relationships.

dead_ends.action: Always apply 합니다체 to sound 'safe', even among close friends
dead_ends.why_fails: Among close friends, family of similar age, or in casual messaging, 합니다체 feels bureaucratic and distant — may read as the speaker holding the listener at arm's length after establishing closeness. It's also strange in casual group chats.

dead_ends.action: Drop honorifics and titles (님, 씨, 선배, 과장님) once rapport is established
dead_ends.why_fails: Even friendly colleagues often keep 님/씨/직함 indefinitely. Dropping them without explicit invitation (말 놓으세요) signals presumption and can be read as rude, especially cross-generation or cross-rank.

workarounds.action: Default to 해요체 for general messaging and 합니다체 for formal/business written communication. Use honorific titles (김부장님, 이과장님) plus 님 with given names for colleagues.
Mirror the register the Korean counterpart uses. If they write in 합니다체, reply in 합니다체; if 해요체, match. Only step down to 반말 after explicit invitation.

When translating, always ask the user about the relationship (colleague? boss? same age? friend?) before choosing speech level. Generic output without this context will be wrong roughly half the time.
